name: BrowserGym and AgentLab evaluation harness
maintenance_status: Actively maintained since January 2025. BrowserGym published v0.14.3 on 2026-01-20 after multiple 2025 releases, and AgentLab published v0.4.2 on 2026-01-20. Recent BrowserGym work added WebArena Lite, WebArena Verified, OpenApps integration, fixes, and AgentLab API preparation. Both repositories also have active issue and pull-request queues.
license: BrowserGym and AgentLab are Apache-2.0 licensed. That permissive license is compatible with use and modification inside ApplyPilot's AGPL-3.0 codebase, subject to preserving the Apache license, notices, attribution, and any notices for separately installed benchmark dependencies. Individual benchmark websites, datasets, and containers can have separate licenses and terms.
pricing_model: Open-source, self-hosted evaluation software with no BrowserGym or AgentLab usage fee. Costs are local or cloud browser compute, benchmark hosting and reset infrastructure, model tokens, trace storage, engineering for custom ATS tasks and graders, and human review of ambiguous traces. AgentLab supports OpenAI, Azure, OpenRouter, and self-hosted TGI, so model billing follows the selected provider.
marginal_cost_per_attempt: [uncertain] A deterministic synthetic task can have approximately zero software and model cost, plus local browser compute. An agent-backed task adds the selected model's token cost for every observation/action loop. A planning range of $0.01-$0.25 per synthetic episode is plausible for short local forms, but no ApplyPilot ATS task has been metered and long multimodal episodes can cost materially more.
projected_cost_per_verified_apply: [uncertain] This is an evaluation harness, not an application route, so it does not directly produce verified applications. Its value is avoided live-canary failures and earlier rejection of expensive routes. The effect on ApplyPilot's all-in cost per verified apply cannot be calculated until synthetic scores are joined to route-tagged live outcomes, fallback spend, and maintenance cost.
billing_granularity: There is no BrowserGym or AgentLab billing unit. Browser and Ray workers consume resources for each job, idle warm workers consume RAM, traces consume storage, and benchmark services may remain running between studies. Model providers bill by their own token, request, subscription, or hosted-endpoint units. WebArena resets can add roughly five minutes before evaluation according to AgentLab documentation.
model_cost: [uncertain] Zero for deterministic adapter regression tasks. For a general web agent, cost equals all prompts, DOM or accessibility observations, optional screenshots, reasoning output, and retries across the episode. AgentLab unifies model access but does not make inference free or publish an ApplyPilot token budget; measure input, cached input, output, and retry tokens per task and model.
infrastructure_cost: [uncertain] BrowserGym launches Chromium through Playwright and each concurrent AgentLab job needs browser RAM and CPU; self-hosted benchmark environments may add Docker services and reset time. AgentLab reports that 10-50 jobs can often run on one computer depending on RAM, but ApplyPilot's Windows fleet capacity, power, storage, and optional Ray overhead have not been measured.
human_recovery_cost: [uncertain] Synthetic tasks should avoid login and OTP labor by using seeded accounts or fixtures. Human time remains necessary to author tasks, adjudicate grader errors, inspect ambiguous traces, refresh fixtures after browser or ATS changes, and supervise any later live canary. No ApplyPilot operator-minute study exists.
adapter_maintenance_tco: [uncertain] Medium initial cost: implement an AbstractBrowserTask-compatible synthetic ATS suite, fixtures, reset logic, success graders, hostile-page cases, route metadata export, and CI packaging. Ongoing cost tracks changes in BrowserGym/Playwright and the production adapters being modeled. The harness can reduce duplicated test machinery, but hours and amortized cost per verified apply are unmeasured.
verified_completion_rate: [uncertain] No BrowserGym or AgentLab benchmark measures positively verified job applications, and no ApplyPilot-specific suite exists. Built-in benchmark rewards cannot be treated as an ATS completion rate. Establish a matched synthetic completion rate first, then validate its predictive relationship to route-tagged live canaries before using it as a promotion gate.
failure_modes: Synthetic pages or graders do not represent real ATS widgets, conditional logic, uploads, redirects, or confirmation evidence
A task reward reports success even though required fields, answer provenance, or exactly-once submission constraints were violated
BrowserGym, Playwright, browser, model, benchmark container, or live benchmark changes reduce reproducibility
Ray jobs hang, time out, exhaust RAM, or leave benchmark state contaminated between tasks
Shared WebArena-style state creates ordering dependencies or requires slow instance resets
Dynamic live-web tasks vary by geography, language, account state, or site changes
Full DOM, accessibility trees, screenshots, or traces expose candidate data or secrets
An unrestricted Python action or hostile page content causes prompt injection, arbitrary navigation, or unsafe tool use
Headless and headed modes or different viewport settings produce materially different task behavior
A benchmark score is overfit and fails to predict cost, quality, or verified completion in live canaries
crash_recovery: AgentLab can load a saved Study, find incomplete or errored tasks, and relaunch them; its Ray backend can terminate jobs that exceed a timeout. ExpResult and study directories preserve per-job results for diagnosis. This is appropriate before irreversible actions. A restarted episode is not transaction replay, so any synthetic or live task that might have crossed submit must persist submit_started and quarantine rather than automatically rerun.
exactly_once_submit_safety: Not built in as a cross-task guarantee. BrowserGym executes actions and task-specific validation, while AgentLab schedules jobs and retries incomplete work. ApplyPilot must create a grader and state machine that records a durable pre-submit checkpoint, allows exactly one submit action, detects duplicate requests, refuses automatic retry after an ambiguous click, and treats duplicate or false-positive success as a zero-tolerance benchmark failure.
verification_evidence_tier: Custom tasks can grade exact synthetic server state or response IDs as the strongest evidence, then known confirmation DOM and URL, with screenshots and action traces as supporting artifacts. Built-in scalar reward alone is insufficient for ApplyPilot. The ATS suite should emit the same evidence tiers used by production: submission ID or response, confirmation DOM, email fixture, screenshot, and explicit inference-only status.
verification_latency: Synthetic server-state and DOM graders can run synchronously after each action and at episode end; delayed-email behavior can be simulated with a controlled queue and reconciliation clock. [uncertain] End-to-end p50 and p95 latency for an ApplyPilot suite are unknown until tasks, browser startup mode, model, artifact retention, and reset policy are implemented.
headless_headed_parity: BrowserGym exposes a headless option but documents that disabling headless or changing viewport can change task behavior and difficulty. [uncertain] No ApplyPilot ATS parity data exists. Run identical seeds in headless Chromium, headed Chromium, and the production CDP-attached mode where feasible, then fail certification when required-field, action, challenge, or confirmation outcomes differ.
python_integration: Strong. BrowserGym exposes Gymnasium environments and BrowserGym/AgentLab are Python packages; AgentLab requires Python 3.11 or 3.12 and provides Python APIs for studies, custom agents, results, and analysis. ApplyPilot can wrap its existing Python deterministic adapters and agent controller behind an AgentArgs implementation and export results into its database.
playwright_cdp_reuse: Partial. BrowserGym itself uses Playwright and accepts Chromium and BrowserContext keyword arguments, so selectors, page concepts, screenshots, and some fixture utilities are reusable. Its core BrowserEnv launches a new Chromium browser and creates a new context on reset; it does not natively attach to ApplyPilot's already-running CDP browser/profile. Production CDP reuse needs a custom environment or should remain outside the synthetic harness.
migration_effort: [uncertain] Medium for a useful synthetic harness and large for production-path substitution. A first phase needs package integration, a custom ATS task class, local fixture server, typed task catalog, graders, one ApplyPilot agent wrapper, result export, and CI. Replacing current browser/profile orchestration would require invasive BrowserEnv changes and is not recommended.
ats_fit: Strong as an extensible framework for synthetic Ashby, Greenhouse, Lever, Workable, Workday, and long-tail form replicas because new benchmarks can inherit AbstractBrowserTask and define setup and validation. It has no bundled ATS benchmark, application schema, resume handling, login workflow, or confirmation-email grader. Fit therefore depends entirely on ApplyPilot-authored fixtures and task validity.
deterministic_coverage: [uncertain] The harness can test every deterministic adapter and synthetic variant that ApplyPilot encodes, but that is test-suite coverage, not production route coverage. Initial coverage should include all currently supported deterministic fields and known failure fixtures; the share of live jobs represented must be calculated from ATS, tenant, form-version, and question-shape buckets.
escape_rate: [uncertain] In the harness, escape is the share of tasks that leave the deterministic adapter for semantic, vision, premium-agent, or human handling. BrowserGym can measure this only after ApplyPilot encodes route transitions and terminal dispositions. No current rate exists; report it by ATS and form complexity rather than averaging across only runnable tasks.
control_plane_overhead: Moderate for agent evaluations: BrowserGym extracts screenshot, DOM snapshot, merged accessibility tree, element properties, URL, page list, last action, errors, and elapsed time after steps; AgentLab serializes experiment configuration and results and may use Ray workers. Deterministic adapters can bypass most model context but still incur environment extraction unless a lean custom observation path is used.
observability_joinability: Good after an ApplyPilot exporter is added. AgentLab study directories, ExpResult, summary dataframes, screenshots, actions, step information, seeds, agent arguments, benchmark names, and reproducibility metadata can be joined to attempt_id, route, ATS, fixture version, adapter commit, model, token usage, and production canary cohort. There is no native ApplyPilot database join key.
persistent_profiles: Weak by default for production authentication. BrowserGym creates a fresh browser and context during reset and closes them afterward, although Playwright context options can seed controlled storage state. Use synthetic accounts and fixture storage states in benchmarks. Do not point it at a user's default Chrome profile; production profile locking, cloning, versioning, and ownership remain ApplyPilot responsibilities.
existing_session_attach: Not a documented first-class BrowserGym workflow. The core environment launches Chromium rather than calling connect_over_cdp, and closes its own browser on reset or close. [uncertain] A custom environment could attach to an existing session, but lifecycle, active-tab selection, profile locks, and ambiguous submit recovery would need engineering and tests.
login_and_otp: Use seeded synthetic accounts, deterministic OTP fixtures, and explicit owner-takeover simulation to test state transitions without real credentials. BrowserGym can pause for a user message in interactive mode, but AgentLab does not provide an ApplyPilot inbox relay or secure OTP service. Real login, passkey, CAPTCHA, or suspicious-auth prompts must remain supervised and outside unattended benchmark automation.
extension_support: No documented BrowserGym extension-management layer. Chromium launch arguments and context options may permit controlled extensions in a custom setup, but [uncertain] compatibility with AgentLab workers and benchmark isolation is not established. The synthetic suite should require no extension; test narrowly permissioned owner-takeover extensions separately if production depends on them.
session_data_boundary: Self-hosting keeps browser state, screenshots, traces, prompts, and results on ApplyPilot-controlled machines except model requests sent to the configured provider and traffic to any live benchmark site. AgentLab results default under AGENTLAB_EXP_ROOT. Synthetic tasks should use fake candidate data; redact cookies, CSRF tokens, resumes, emails, and screenshots before external model calls or retained artifacts.
required_field_completeness: Implement this as a first-class task invariant, not merely a final reward. Each synthetic form should expose a server-side expected required-field set, conditional branches, hidden and custom controls, and post-fill validation. The grader must fail if the route submits with any missing, stale, duplicated, or invalid required value and should report unmapped_required separately.
answer_provenance: BrowserGym does not enforce provenance. The ApplyPilot agent wrapper and task server should tag every answer with profile, resume, approved-answer, policy, verifier, or human source; reject generated claims without an approved source; and grade both value correctness and provenance. Hostile page text must never become an authoritative answer source.
unsupported_question_fallback: Create benchmark cases for novel free text, sensitive questions, contradictory prompts, and missing evidence. Passing behavior is a structured unresolved-field disposition or approved human/verifier handoff before submit, with no guessed answer. Record fallback class, cost, latency, and whether previous deterministic work is safely reused.
prompt_injection_resistance: BrowserGym includes broad action primitives and its documented Python action can execute code with the active Playwright page, which the docs label unsafe. Use a restricted HighLevelActionSet, ATS origin allowlists, no arbitrary Python or shell action for model-controlled agents, fake secrets, egress controls, and DoomArena-style hostile-page fixtures. Grade secret disclosure, off-domain navigation, instruction override, and submit-boundary violations.
site_permission_and_terms: Synthetic locally hosted ATS replicas are the preferred use because they avoid live-site side effects and can be licensed and rate-controlled by ApplyPilot. Built-in live or self-hosted benchmarks have their own setup and terms. BrowserGym's repository warns that it is research software rather than a consumer product; capability does not authorize unattended applications or bypass of login, CAPTCHA, access controls, or site restrictions.
irreversible_action_guard: Represent submit as a separately permissioned action available only after a complete typed plan and durable checkpoint. Synthetic tasks should count server-side submit calls, inject crashes before and after acceptance, and fail on more than one call, fallback after ambiguous acceptance, or success without strong evidence. Live canaries must retain ApplyPilot's independent enablement gates and stop-loss controls.
concurrency: AgentLab uses Ray for parallel experiments and documents that 10-50 jobs can often run on one computer depending on RAM. WebArena-style state dependencies reduce safe concurrency, and AgentLab does not currently support evaluation across multiple benchmark instances. Synthetic ATS fixtures should isolate state per attempt and begin with a conservative worker count based on measured browser memory.
startup_latency: [uncertain] Cold startup includes Python worker setup, Playwright and Chromium launch, fixture navigation, and optional model initialization. Warm Ray workers reduce process overhead, but BrowserGym still creates a browser for environment reset. ApplyPilot p50 and p95 startup latency are unmeasured; record worker-ready, browser-ready, task-ready, and first-action timestamps.
warm_session_reuse: AgentLab can keep parallel workers active across jobs, but BrowserGym's standard reset tears down and recreates task, context, chat, and browser. Reuse model clients, fixture services, downloaded browser binaries, task catalogs, and analysis caches; do not assume authenticated browser or tab reuse. A lean custom environment may reuse a browser while creating isolated contexts if profiling justifies it.
tracing_and_replay: Strong experiment inspection but not deterministic transaction replay. AgentLab ExpResult exposes per-step screenshots, actions, and step information; AgentXray visualizes ongoing or completed traces, and studies can reload incomplete results. BrowserGym can record viewport video. Add Playwright traces, network and console capture, fixture server logs, and model token records. Replay only against synthetic fixtures and never blindly replay submit on a live ATS.
deployment_modes: Local Python environment, self-hosted workstation or fleet machine, containerized benchmark services, and Ray-backed parallel execution are supported patterns. Built-in benchmarks range from static files and datasets to self-hosted Docker services, hosted demo instances, and live web. There is no required managed BrowserGym cloud. A CI image with pinned browsers and local synthetic ATS services is the recommended ApplyPilot mode.
browser_version_matrix: BrowserGym core currently drives Playwright Chromium; its public setup installs Chromium and the core environment launches pw.chromium. Built-in BrowserGym is not a Firefox, WebKit, WebDriver BiDi, or generic CDP compatibility harness. [uncertain] Cross-browser ATS certification would need separate Playwright tests or custom environments. Pin Python, BrowserGym, AgentLab, Playwright, browser build, OS image, viewport, locale, and timezone in every result.
canary_stop_loss: Gate progression from unit fixtures to synthetic BrowserGym studies, then shadow on real pages without submit, then a tiny unique-job live canary. Enforce per-step, episode, token, dollar, and wall-clock caps; zero duplicate submissions, false APPLIED states, secret leaks, wrong answers, or challenge interaction; and pause a route on regression versus its pinned baseline or on any ambiguous submit.
benchmark_design: Build an ApplyPilot benchmark package with matched ATS-stratified synthetic tasks for Ashby, Greenhouse, Lever, Workable, Workday, and long-tail patterns. Cover standard and conditional required fields, custom selects, dates, uploads, consent, iframes, redirects, validation errors, expired jobs, auth and OTP handoff, hostile instructions, delayed confirmation, duplicate clicks, and crashes around submit. Run deterministic, semantic-agent, vision-agent, and human-fallback routes on identical seeded cases with at least three repeats per case/model configuration. Hold out fixture variants, pin all software and task versions, and correlate synthetic metrics with later route-tagged live canaries.
route_funnel: Export discovered -> eligible -> assigned route -> environment ready -> plan complete -> fallback requested -> required fields complete -> pre-submit checkpoint -> submit attempted -> server accepted -> DOM confirmed -> delayed evidence reconciled -> final disposition. Separate task coverage from completion among attempted tasks, and retain failure class, evidence tier, model and token cost, action count, elapsed time, and synthetic/live cohort on every row.
step_context_budget: [uncertain] Define route-specific caps before experiments: deterministic routes should use zero model observations; semantic routes should cap steps, DOM or accessibility-tree characters/tokens, screenshots, tool calls, output tokens, and retries; vision routes should separately meter image count and resolution. BrowserGym exposes rich observations, so an uncapped default can be expensive. Actual limits require pilot distributions.
cache_replay_yield: [uncertain] Measure how often a previously validated deterministic plan, locator template, approved answer, or action prefix succeeds on a held-out fixture without a model call. Key cache entries by ATS, tenant, form schema digest, adapter version, browser version, and fixture version. Invalidate on mismatch and never cache or replay the final submit. No warm-hit or savings rate exists yet.
confidence_intervals: [uncertain] No ApplyPilot result distribution exists. Report Wilson 95% intervals for verified synthetic completion, fallback, ambiguous result, provenance violation, and field-completeness failure; bootstrap cost, duration, tokens, and actions; stratify by ATS and complexity; and use paired comparisons on identical seeds. For zero duplicate or false-positive events, publish a one-sided upper bound rather than claiming zero risk.
historical_bucket_impact: [uncertain] The harness should be aimed at expensive agent_browser_runtime, crash_unconfirmed, unsupported-field, and ambiguous-confirmation buckets, while also testing expired and auth/challenge stop behavior. It will not itself remove historical spend. Calculate impact only after mapping each synthetic failure to ApplyPilot failure_class and measuring whether benchmark-gated routes improve all-in cost per successful apply in live cohorts.
recommendation: Adopt for synthetic and shadow evaluation, not as a production browser route. Implement a small ApplyPilot-specific BrowserGym package and use AgentLab for seeded parallel studies, timeout handling, trace analysis, and matched route comparisons. Start with Greenhouse, Ashby, and Workable-like fixtures plus submit-crash and hostile-page cases. Keep existing Playwright/CDP profiles and deterministic adapters in production, export benchmark results into ApplyPilot attempt IDs and route metadata, and require demonstrated correlation with live canary outcomes before using synthetic scores as promotion gates.
